facts.text: The World Health Organization estimates that about 1 in 100 children were diagnosed with autism between 2012 and 2021.
Autism diagnoses have risen since the 1990s, largely due to broader diagnostic criteria, greater awareness, and wider access to assessment.
Autism is classified as a neurodevelopmental disorder.
Approximately 85% of autistic people need support with independent living in adulthood.
Mortality among autistic people is approximately twice that of the general population.
Among children diagnosed with autism before age 6, about 9 in 10 continue to meet diagnostic criteria one year later.
Developing spoken language before age six is associated with a higher likelihood of independent living in adulthood for autistic people.
Having an IQ above 50 is associated with a higher likelihood of independent living in adulthood for autistic people.
Autistic people are significantly more likely to identify as LGBTQ than the general population.
Autistic people are significantly more likely to be non-theistic or non-religious than the general population.
There is no cure for autism.
Genetics is considered likely the largest contributing factor to autism.
Autism is increasingly thought to have distinct, often co-occurring causes for its core aspects rather than a single cause.
Autistic adults are more likely to be unemployed than the general population.
Autistic adults have greater mental health service needs than the general population.
Some researchers argue that the perception of lessened autistic features over time is due to masking — hiding autistic traits to avoid stigma.
Autism support needs range from minimal to requiring 24-hour care, reflecting the spectrum nature of the condition.
Most professionals believe that race, ethnicity, and socioeconomic background have limited effect on the occurrence of autism.
Restricted and repetitive behaviors are among the most stable core diagnostic symptoms of autism over time.
Prenatal and perinatal history are among the factors identified as potential contributors to autism.
The neurodiversity movement supports interventions aimed at enhancing functional communication rather than altering core autistic features.
Autism was long presumed to have a single cause at the genetic, cognitive, and neural levels, but this view has been revised.
Signs of autism are in most cases first observable in infancy or early childhood and remain throughout life.
Autistic people may be significantly disabled in some respects but average or superior in others.
The term 'stimming' refers to restricted or repetitive patterns of behavior commonly associated with autism.
